prompt:
Your organization has a Microsoft 365 E5 subscription.
You need to ensure that a third-party cloud service can authenticate to Microsoft Entra.
What should you configure?
explanation:
D .an app registration.
An app registration creates a service principal in Azure AD (Microsoft Entra) and supplies the credentials
(client ID, tenant ID, secret or certificate) that external SaaS applications use to sign in to Microsoft 365.
External services that need to authenticate to Entra must be registered as “enterprise applications” and
granted an appropriate permission (e.g., Application Permissions such as User.Read.All or Directory.Read.All).
Once registered, the service can use OAuth 2.0 client-credential flow (or certificate-based authentication) to
obtain an access token, enabling secure programmatic access without interactive user involvement.

Why the other options are not suitable
A – Microsoft 365 Copilot connector – This is used to expose Copilot data to external tools; it does not
provide a generic authentication mechanism for arbitrary third-party services.
B – Multifactor authentication (MFA) – MFA protects user sign-ins, but external services typically run
non-interactive flows and cannot present a second factor; they rely on service-principal authentication rather
than user-based MFA.
C – Conditional Access policy – Conditional Access enforces policies (e.g., location, device compliance) on
sign-ins, but it does not establish the credential set needed for an app to authenticate; it only governs access
after authentication has occurred.
Therefore, configuring an app registration is the precise step that enables a third-party cloud service to
authenticate to Microsoft Entra.
References
App registration and service principal overview: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-
directory/develop/app-objects-and-service-principals
Authentication flows for daemon applications (client-credential flow): https://learn.microsoft.com/en-
us/azure/active-directory/develop/v2-oauth2-client-creds-grant-flow
These links are current as of 2025 and illustrate the concepts referenced above.

prompt:
Your organization has a Microsoft 365 subscription.
You need to evaluate your organization’s Identity Secure Score.
Which two factors affect the score? Each correct answer presents a complete the solution.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
B – the number of global administrators
C – passwords that are never expired
Why these are the right factors
Identity Secure Score evaluates the strength of an organization’s identity posture. Reducing the count of
global administrators lowers privileged access risk, directly improving the score.
Enforcing passwords that do not expire (i.e., requiring periodic change) is a recommended control; leaving
passwords permanently unchanged raises the risk of credential misuse and therefore negatively impacts the
score.
Why the other options are not considered
A – SharePoint site permissions: While proper permission hygiene is important for overall security, it is not a
direct component of the Identity Secure Score calculation.
D – The location of the users: User location may affect compliance considerations, but it is not a parameter
that influences the Secure Score’s identity-related metrics.
References
Microsoft Secure Score – Identity page
Password policies and expiration in Microsoft 365

prompt:

You need to review the impact of a recent phishing incident that targeted email users.
What should you use?
explanation:
Why option A is the correct choice
The Microsoft Defender portal (Defender for Office 365) provides native capabilities for investigating phishing
incidents, including email trace, quarantine/release, threat-intelligence reports, and the
“Investigate & respond” workflow.
It surfaces malicious-email indicators, malicious-sender reputation, attachment scanning results (Safe
Attachments), and URL reputation (Safe Links), enabling you to assess the scope and impact of a targeted
phishing campaign.
All remediation actions (e.g., message quarantine, user notifications, policy adjustments) are centrally
managed from this portal.
Why the other options are less suitable
Microsoft 365 admin center – a general-purpose admin hub that does not expose the granular email-threat
analytics needed for forensic investigation.
Microsoft Entra admin center – focuses on identity and access management (users, groups, SSO, conditional
access) and does not contain mail-content analysis or threat-intelligence data.
Microsoft Exchange admin center – manages mailbox configuration, transport rules, and mail flow but lacks
the integrated phishing-investigation tools (e.g., Safe Attachments, Safe Links, incident response playbooks)
that are only available through the Defender portal.
References
Microsoft Defender for Office 365 – Investigation & response: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-
365/security/office-365-security/anti-phishing-protection
Investigate phishing emails using the Security & Compliance Center: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-
us/exchange/security-and-compliance/anti-phishing

prompt:
Your organization has a Microsoft 365 subscription.
You create a security group named Group1 and assign a Microsoft 365 E3 license to the group.
You discover that a user named User1 does NOT have access to the Microsoft 365 E3 features.
You need to ensure that User1 can access all the Microsoft 365 E3 features.
Which two actions can you perform? Each correct answer presents a complete solution.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
Why the correct selections are required
Add User1 to Group1 (A) – When a license is assigned to a security group, all members of that group inherit
the license. Adding the user to the group makes the E3 license effective for that account, enabling access to
the full suite of E3 services.
Assign a license to User1 (D) – Even if the user is a member of a licensed group, the license must also be
explicitly granted to the user object in Azure AD. Direct assignment ensures the user has a valid license
assignment, which is required for feature activation and compliance reporting.
Why the other options are not appropriate
Assign a Conditional Access policy to Group1 (B) – Conditional Access controls sign-in risk and access based
on policies, but it does not grant or modify license entitlement. License availability is independent of sign-in
conditions.
Assign a Conditional Access policy to User1 (C) – Similar to the above, a Conditional Access policy can
enforce sign-in requirements, yet it cannot create or deliver the E3 license capabilities that are missing.
Summary
To resolve the lack of E3 feature access for User1, you must (1) place the user in the correctly licensed
security group and (2) assign an E3 license directly to the user. These steps together provide the necessary
entitlement for all Microsoft 365 E3 services.
References
Assigning licenses to users or groups: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/enterprise-
users/licensing-methods#licensing-by-group
Understanding Microsoft 365 license assignments: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-
365/compliance/manage-licenses?view=o365-worldwide

options.label: Assign a Conditional Access policy to Group1.
Assign a Conditional Access policy to User1.

The correct choice is B – the Microsoft Entra admin center.

reduction; it does not host authentication audit logs.

not expose sign-in attempt records.
D. Microsoft 365 admin center – Offers high-level service management and user administration but does not
provide granular sign-in failure details; deeper audit data lives in Entra.
Therefore, to view failed sign-in attempts you must query the Sign-in logs in the Microsoft Entra admin
center.
References
View sign-in logs in Microsoft Entra admin center
Troubleshoot sign-in problems in Microsoft 365

prompt: What can you use to block a user account automatically when a risky sigh-in is detected?
explanation:
Why B – Microsoft Entra ID Protection is the correct choice
Microsoft Entra ID Protection automatically evaluates each authentication attempt and can trigger a
risk-based response, such as requiring re-authentication or, when the risk level exceeds a configured
threshold, automatically disabling the user account until the risk is mitigated.
The service integrates directly with Azure AD sign-in logs, applies machine-learning models to identify
anomalous behavior, and can enforce policy actions (including account lockout) in real time without manual
intervention.
This behavior matches the exam-style requirement of “blocking a user account automatically when a risky
sign-in is detected.”
Why the other options are less suitable
A. Microsoft Defender for Identity – Provides threat detection for on-premises directories and can surface
suspicious sign-ins, but its primary purpose is visibility and investigation; it does not natively enforce
automatic account lockout as part of the Azure AD risk policy workflow.
C. Microsoft Defender for Office 365 – Focuses on protecting email and collaboration workloads from
phishing, malware, and other content-based threats; it has no role in managing Azure AD authentication
events or automatic account blockouts.
D. Microsoft Entra Privileged Identity Management (PIM) – Manages eligibility and activation of privileged
roles; it can impose time-bound access, but it does not evaluate sign-in risk nor automatically suspend
accounts based on risky sign-in detections.
Therefore, only Microsoft Entra ID Protection fully satisfies the scenario of automatically blocking a user
when a risky sign-in is detected.
References
Microsoft Entra ID Protection – automatic account lockout: https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/identity-
protection/how-to-identity-protection-risk-policy-account-lockout
Secure sign-in detections and automated responses: https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/identity-
protection/overview-identity-protection#risk-detection-and-responses

prompt: Which statement accurately describes authorization in Microsoft 365?
explanation:
Why option C is the correct description of authorization in Microsoft 365
Authorization is the step that determines whether an authenticated identity has the rights to access a
particular resource (e.g., a mailbox, SharePoint site, or Teams channel).
It evaluates the permissions, role assignments, or entitlement policies that have been granted to the identity;
only after this check can the resource be accessed.
In the Microsoft 365 security model, authorization follows authentication. Authentication confirms who the
user is; authorization answers what that user may do.

Why the other options are inaccurate
Option A describes authentication: verifying that an identity matches its claimed credentials.
Option B describes a type of multifactor authentication or additional verification, not authorization.
Option D refers to identity federation or external identity verification, which is a process used before a user is
even authenticated within Microsoft 365.
Key point: Authorization is fundamentally about access control decisions—the “allowed-to-use”
determination rather than credential validation or additional verification steps.
References
Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) – Authorization and access control concepts:
https://learn.microsoft.com/entra/identity/access-management
Microsoft 365 security fundamentals – Authorization vs. Authentication:
https://learn.microsoft.com/microsoft-365/security/office-365-security/authorization-and-authentication

options.label: a process to verify whether an identity is who or what they claim to be
a process to require additional authentication methods before an identity can access resources
a process to verify whether an identity is allowed to access a resource
a process to validate an identity from an external system

prompt:
You are reviewing your company’s security policies as part of a Zero Trust strategy.
Which statement accurately describes the Zero Trust principles?
explanation:
Justification
Option B – Zero Trust assumes breach and verifies each request.
This is the core definition of Zero Trust: security is not based on location or network perimeter but on
continuous verification of every access request, regardless of origin. It aligns with Microsoft’s guidance that
“never trust, always verify” is the fundamental principle for modern security architectures.
Option A – Minimizes authentication prompts to enhance user experience.
While usability is important, Zero Trust does not aim to reduce the number of verification steps. Instead, it may
increase prompts for sensitive actions to ensure each request is authenticated and authorized.
Option C – Treats all requests from the corporate network as trustworthy.
Zero Trust explicitly rejects the notion that internal traffic is automatically trusted. Even traffic originating
inside the corporate network must be verified before access is granted.
Option D – Removes the need to regularly review and adjust access permissions.
Continuous evaluation of permissions is a cornerstone of Zero Trust. Access rights must be reviewed and
adjusted frequently to reflect changes in user roles, risk levels, and threat landscape.
References
Microsoft Zero Trust documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/security/zero-trust
Zero Trust security principles: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/zero-trust/

options.label: Zero Trust enhances the user experience by minimizing authentication prompts.
Zero Trust assumes breach and verifies each request.
Zero Trust treats all requests from your corporate network as trustworthy.
Zero Trust removes the need to regularly review and adjust access permissions.

endpoint telemetry across Windows 11 devices. Microsoft Defender for Endpoint provides continuous endpoint
detection and response (EDR), threat analytics, and built-in incident investigation capabilities, making it the
only option that directly addresses endpoint-centric security monitoring.

endpoint telemetry or investigation tools.

but it is not designed for device-level incident response on Windows 11.

policy violations; it lacks the endpoint security data and response features needed for alert investigation.
Microsoft Defender for Endpoint integrates with Windows 11 diagnostics, collects security events, and offers
investigation graphs, threat visualization, and automated remediation—exactly what is required for
investigating alerts on these devices.

that flows through AI-enabled workloads such as Copilot. It continuously scans interaction logs, surfaces
usage patterns, and recommends policy actions (labeling, encryption, access controls) tailored to the
sensitivity of the content. This directly satisfies the requirement to identify where sensitive content is being
used during Copilot interactions, analyze usage patterns, and provide protection recommendations.
Microsoft Viva Insights focuses on employee wellbeing, productivity, and privacy analytics at a high-level,
without deep content-level inspection of AI-generated interactions. It does not provide the granular
data-loss-prevention or classification needed for Copilot-specific risk mitigation.
Microsoft Security Copilot is an AI-assisted security analyst that helps security teams investigate threats
and respond to incidents. It operates on security telemetry and alerts, not on the detailed inspection of
user-generated content within Copilot sessions.
Microsoft Purview Insider Risk Management detects risky insider activities (e.g., data exfiltration, policy
violations) across the tenant, but its scope is broader and not specifically tuned to the mechanics of Copilot
usage. It lacks the AI-content awareness and recommendation engine that DSPM for AI offers for this
particular scenario.
Therefore, the only solution that aligns precisely with the described task is the Microsoft Purview DSPM for
AI solution.

prompt:
Your organization has a Microsoft 365 subscription that contains a user named User1.
User1 plans to leave your company in two weeks.
You need to capture the activity of User1 to identify whether the user is exfiltrating data.
Which Microsoft Purview solution should you use?
explanation:
Justification for the correct answer: Insider Risk Management (Option C)
Purpose-built for employee-related risk – Insider Risk Management (IRM) is designed to surface risky

activities that originate from users, such as abnormal file downloads, excessive sharing, or large-scale data
exports. It correlates signals from Microsoft 365 logs (e.g., SharePoint, OneDrive, Teams) with policy-based
detections.
Detects exfiltration behavior – The IRM engine includes pre-defined and customizable sensitive-info types
and alerts that flag suspicious data-movement patterns, giving administrators the visibility needed to
intervene before data leaves the tenant.
Automated evidence collection – When an exfiltration-related alert is triggered, IRM provides detailed
activity timelines, file metadata, and user context, enabling rapid investigation without needing to build
custom scripts.
Why the other options are less suitable
Communication Compliance (A) – Focuses on monitoring and governing communication content for
compliance and policy violations (e.g., harassment, discrimination). It does not provide the granular
data-exfiltration insights required to spot large-scale file copying or aggregation.
Data Security Posture Management (B) – Primarily assesses the security configuration of data stores (e.g.,
permission hygiene, insecure sharing settings) and helps remediate misconfigurations, but it lacks continuous
behavioral monitoring of a specific departing user’s activity.
Data Lifecycle Management (D) – Manages how long data is retained, archived, or destroyed based on
policies; it is not intended for real-time detection of suspicious user actions or exfiltration signals.
Therefore, Insider Risk Management is the optimal Purview solution for capturing and analyzing a departing
user’s activity to identify potential data exfiltration.
References
Microsoft Purview Insider Risk Management – Overview: https://learn.microsoft.com/purview/insider-risk-
management
Detect and investigate risky user activity with Insider Risk Management:
https://learn.microsoft.com/purview/insider-risk-management-investigate-activities

prompt:
Your organization has a Microsoft 365 subscription.
All users are assigned Microsoft 365 Copilot licenses.
Some users report receiving Copilot responses that contain information from a Microsoft SharePoint site named
Finance. The users report that the information is commercially sensitive.
You need to prevent Copilot from providing responses that contain information from the Finance site.
What should you do?
explanation:
Why option A is the correct choice
Information Barriers (IBs) are a feature of Microsoft Purview that let administrators define policies which
block or restrict the sharing of content between specific groups or sites. By configuring an IB that isolates the
Finance SharePoint site from the rest of the tenant, the content stored there will not be exposed to Copilot’s

indexing engine, ensuring that Copilot cannot retrieve or surface commercially-sensitive information from
that site.
This approach directly addresses the requirement to prevent Copilot from delivering responses that contain
data from the Finance site while keeping the broader permissions model intact for legitimate users.
Why the other options are unsuitable
Option B – Defender data connector – Data connectors in Microsoft Defender are used to ingest logs and
alerts into Azure Sentinel or Defender for Cloud. They have no role in controlling Copilot’s content visibility or
in establishing data-access boundaries, so they cannot stop Copilot from accessing or exposing Finance-site
documents.
Option C – Conditional Access policy – Conditional Access governs sign-in and authentication conditions (e.g.,
MFA, device compliance). It does not regulate which files Copilot can read or display; it only influences user
access to services after authentication, making it ineffective for content-level protection.
Option D – Changing permissions on the Finance site – Modifying site permissions can restrict user access,
but Copilot’s content ingestion is based on the SharePoint admin-level exposure settings, not individual user
permissions. Simply adjusting permissions may still leave the site discoverable by Copilot, offering no
guaranteed barrier to sensitive data leakage.
Implementation summary
1. Identify the security group that should never receive Finance-site data.
2. In the Microsoft Purview portal, create an Information Barrier policy targeting that group and the
Finance site.
3. Apply the IB policy, then synchronize the change (usually within a few minutes).
4. Verify that Copilot queries to the Finance site now return “no data available” for blocked groups.
References
Create and configure an Information Barrier policy in Microsoft Purview
Control Copilot access to sensitive content with sensitivity labels and IB policies

options.label: From Microsoft Purview, create an Information Barrier (IB) policy.
From Microsoft Defender, create a data connector.
From Microsoft Entra, create a Conditional Access policy.
From the Finance site, configure permissions.

The SharePoint admin center provides detailed view and audit capabilities for sharing activity across

users, which is exactly what the scenario requires.
Why the other options are not appropriate
B. Microsoft Teams admin center – focuses on Teams policies, user management, and app settings; it does
not expose a comprehensive sharing audit for Teams teams. External sharing for Teams is configured through
the Teams admin center, but you cannot retrieve a historical list of shared teams there.
C. Microsoft 365 admin center – offers a high-level overview of license usage, service health, and general
audit logs, but it lacks the granular site-level sharing visibility that the SharePoint admin center provides.
D. Microsoft Defender portal – is geared toward security threats, endpoints, and identity protection; it does
not surface sharing information for SharePoint or Teams sites.
Therefore, to identify which SharePoint sites and Teams teams have been shared with external users, the
SharePoint admin center is the only tool that delivers the required detailed sharing audit.

prompt:
Your organization has a Microsoft 365 E5 subscription.
You need to prevent users from sharing corporate financial data to external users.
What should you use?
explanation:
Justification
Data Loss Prevention (DLP) policies are the only mechanisms that can inspect content for sensitive
information (e.g., financial data patterns, keywords, or templates) and enforce a block on outbound sharing
with external recipients. DLP can be scoped to specific compliance categories, applied to Exchange,
SharePoint, OneDrive, and Teams, and configured to quarantine or reject sharing attempts in real time.
Role groups manage permissions but cannot analyze data content; they only control who can perform actions,
not what data is being shared.
Insider risk management policies focus on detecting and responding to risky user behavior (e.g., unusual
downloads) but do not provide granular, content-level prevention of external sharing of specific data types.

Retention labels control how long data is kept and whether it can be deleted, not how it is shared externally.
They have no impact on outbound sharing controls.
Therefore, to effectively prevent sharing of corporate financial data to external users, a DLP policy targeting
the relevant sensitive information type is the appropriate solution.
References
Microsoft Learn: Data loss prevention (DLP) policies in Microsoft 365
Microsoft Docs: [Create, test, and deploy a DLP policy](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-
365/compliance/create-test-and deploy-dlp-policies)

prompt:
You have a Microsoft SharePoint site named Site1 and a security group named Group1.
You need to prevent all users that currently have access to Site1 from accessing the site content, unless the user is
also a member of Group1.
Which settings should you configure? To answer, select the appropriate settings in the answer area.
explanation:
1. Privacy: Public vs. Private
Current Setting: Public
The Risk: In the Microsoft 365 ecosystem, a Public group site means that everyone within your organization
can discover the site, join it, and read or modify its content without requiring explicit permission from an
owner.
Copilot Impact: Microsoft 365 Copilot respects native user permissions via the Microsoft Graph. Because the
site is set to Public, its documents are open to the entire company. Consequently, when any user triggers a
Copilot prompt, Copilot can discover, index, and surface content from Site1 in its responses.
2. Restrict content from Microsoft 365 Copilot
Current Setting: Off
How to use it: This is a dedicated exclusionary toggle. If an administrator switches this setting to On, it places

a hard compliance block on the site's underlying document libraries. Even if users have access to the site,
Copilot's semantic indexer is completely barred from analyzing its files, preventing the site's data from being
used as a source for AI-generated answers.
3. Restricted Site Access (RSA)
Current Setting: Not set
Why it's a game-changer: This feature is designed to mitigate the risks associated with the Public privacy
setting or loose sharing links. If a site is widely shared but contains sensitive information, clicking Edit under
Restricted site access allows you to specify a designated security group (e.g., Site1-Allowed-Users).
Once configured, only members of that specific security group can access the site or discover its content via
Copilot, completely blocking access for the rest of the organization regardless of broad sharing links or public
group settings.

prompt:

You create a Microsoft Purview sensitivity label named Label1.
You need to ensure that users can apply Label1 to files in Microsoft 365.
What should you use?
explanation:
Why option A is the correct choice
A sensitivity label policy is the mechanism that makes a created sensitivity label (e.g., Label1) available to
users across the organization. The policy must be published to assign the label to users, groups, or the entire
tenant, enabling them to apply the label to files and emails.
Without publishing a label policy, the label exists only in the admin center and cannot be selected by
end-users in apps such as Word, Excel, or Outlook.
The other options serve different purposes:
B. Trainable classifier – Used to discover and classify content based on patterns, not to make a manually
created sensitivity label consumable.
C. Retention label policy – Applies only to retention settings; it does not expose a general sensitivity label for
user-driven classification.
D. Auto-labeling policy – Automates label assignment based on conditions but still requires an underlying
sensitivity label to be defined; it does not by itself “ensure users can apply” the label.
Why the other options are unsuitable
They address classification, retention, or automation, none of which directly provide users with the ability to
manually select and apply a sensitivity label. Only a properly published sensitivity label policy accomplishes
that requirement.

Create and publish a sensitivity label policy

prompt:
Your company has a written compliance policy that requires all emails be retained for seven years, and then
permanently deleted.
Which Microsoft Purview solution should you use?
explanation:
Why the correct answer is B – Data Lifecycle Management
Microsoft Purview Data Lifecycle Management integrates retention policies and auto-deletion actions,
enabling you to define a fixed retention period (e.g., seven years) and automatically purge items once that
period expires.
The service stores retention settings centrally in the Compliance Center, applies them to Exchange,
SharePoint, and OneDrive, and enforces permanent deletion after the configured retention expiration, which
directly satisfies the company’s seven-year-retain-then-destroy policy.
It provides analytics on lifecycle triggers and audit logs to verify compliance with regulatory or internal
retention requirements.
Why the other options are less suitable
A. Information Protection – focuses on classifying and encrypting sensitive content; it does not manage
retention periods or permanent deletion.
C. Data Loss Prevention – governs detection and prevention of data exfiltration and improper sharing, not

prompt:

Excel workbook.

explanation:
Correct option – D. the code interpreter capability
The code interpreter feature enables a Copilot agent to execute custom scripts that read the contents of an
Excel workbook, process the data, and dynamically generate charts or visualizations. By configuring the
interpreter to run Python or Power Automate scripts, the agent can programmatically create visual outputs
that are directly derived from the workbook’s tabular data.
Why option A is unsuitable
The image-generator capability is focused on creating graphics from textual prompts, not on extracting,

analyzing, and visualizing tabular data from Excel. It lacks the ability to read spreadsheet structures or run
calculations required for chart generation.

The Scrum Assistant template is designed for agile project management tasks (e.g., tracking sprints, creating
backlogs). It does not provide data-processing or visualization functions relevant to Excel workbooks.

from CRM and feedback sources. It does not include built-in support for Excel chart creation or
data-transformation operations needed for visualizing workbook data.
Technical alignment
Configuring the code interpreter allows the agent to invoke runtime environments (Python, Power Automate)
that can open the Excel file, manipulate the data model, and output visual charts either as images or as
embedded objects within the Copilot experience. This capability directly satisfies the requirement to produce
data-driven visualizations from an Excel workbook.

prompt:
A user named User1 creates a Microsoft 365 Copilot agent named Agent1 and shares the agent to a user named
User2.
What occurs if an admin blocks Agent1?
explanation:
Technical Justification
When an administrator applies a block on a Copilot agent, the platform removes the agent instance from
every user who previously installed it. This is enforced at the policy level, ensuring that the blocked agent
cannot remain active for any user.
Blocking is a global action; it applies to all users in the tenant. Consequently, the blocked agent becomes
unavailable for installation or use by anyone, regardless of whether they are the original creator or a recipient.
The removal is immediate and automatic—there is no “manual uninstall” step required from end-users. The
system clears the agent’s footprint from user profiles and revokes its visibility in the app store or app bar.
Because the removal is tenant-wide, the original creator (User1) loses access to the agent as well. The creator
can no longer use the agent until it is redeployed or the block is lifted.
Why the other options are incorrect
Option A & B – Imply that Agent1 remains available to User1 and User2 after the admin block, which
contradicts the global removal behavior enforced by the administration console.
Option C – Suggests that only User2 would lose access while User1 retains usage. Administrative blocks are
not scoped to individual users; they affect the entire tenant, so User1 cannot continue using the blocked
agent.
Therefore, the scenario described in option D precisely reflects the system’s behavior: the agent is removed
from all users and becomes undistributable until the block is removed or the agent is redeployed.
References
Microsoft Learn: Manage Microsoft 365 Copilot agents – https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-
365/copilot/manage-agents
Microsoft Docs: Admin control of Copilot agents – https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-
365/copilot/admin-controls-agents

Agent1 is removed from User2, and User1 can continue to use Agent1.

Customization: The Gallery often provides "placeholder" prompts (e.g., [topic], [file]). You can modify these

team.
The "Share to Team" Feature: Within the "Your prompts" tab, each saved prompt card has a Share button.
Selecting this allows you to choose a specific Microsoft 365 Group or Teams team.
Collaborative Library: Once shared, the prompt appears in the "Team prompts" tab for everyone in that
specific team. This ensures that a department (like Marketing or Sales) can use a consistent "best practice"
prompt for recurring tasks.
Permissions: You can manage who has access to these shared prompts and even "Stop Sharing" if the prompt
is no longer relevant to the group.

see a "Copy prompt link" icon.

page. When someone else clicks the link, it opens their Copilot with your exact prompt pre-filled in their text
box, ready to run.

prompt:

explanation:
Statement 1: Surfaces data individual users have permissions to (Yes)
"Microsoft 365 Copilot surfaces organizational data to which individual users have at least view permissions."
Explanation: Copilot respects the existing security model of your organization. It uses a process called
grounding to retrieve information from the Microsoft Graph (emails, chats, and files).
Permission Trimming: Copilot is designed to strictly follow Role-Based Access Control (RBAC). If a user does
not have permission to view a specific document in SharePoint or OneDrive, Copilot cannot "see" it and will not
include information from that document in its response. It essentially acts as the user, with the same access
rights and limitations.
Statement 2: Uses the same underlying controls for data access (Yes)
"Microsoft 365 Copilot uses the same underlying controls for data access as other Microsoft 365 services."

Explanation: Copilot does not create a new security silo or bypass existing rules. It integrates directly with the
Microsoft 365 service boundary.
Security Features: It honors the same protections you already have in place, such as:
Sensitivity Labels: If a file is labeled "Highly Confidential," Copilot respects the encryption and usage rights.
Conditional Access: Copilot follows the same MFA and device compliance policies required for other M365
apps.
Data Loss Prevention (DLP): It adheres to policies that prevent sensitive information from being shared
inappropriately.
Statement 3: Use connectors to retrieve information from third-party data sources (Yes)
"Microsoft 365 Copilot can use connectors to retrieve information from third-party data sources."
Explanation: While Copilot is natively integrated with Microsoft 365 data, it can be extended to "see" data
outside of the Microsoft ecosystem using Microsoft Graph connectors.
How it works: Administrators can configure connectors for external sources like ServiceNow, Jira, Salesforce,
or Google Drive. Once connected, this data is indexed and can be used by Copilot to provide answers that
combine both your internal Microsoft data and your external line-of-business information.

The Microsoft 365 admin center is the central hub for all licensing operations. It lets administrators view

compliance.

so the assigned license becomes active for the user across all subscribed services (Exchange, SharePoint,
Teams, etc.).
The licensing blade includes filters, bulk-assignment capabilities, and audit-trail reporting, all of which are
required to reliably provision a license to a specific account.

protection, retention policies, and data loss prevention. It does not expose licensing functions and therefore
cannot be used to assign a license to a user.

policies, app permissions, chat settings). Licensing is outside its scope; any license assignment made here
would not propagate to other Microsoft 365 services and would not be visible in the broader licensing
inventory.
Conclusion

Only the Microsoft 365 admin center provides the built-in, supported mechanism for assigning and managing
licenses across the entire Microsoft 365 tenant.

prompt:
You use Microsoft 365 Copilot.
You need to schedule a prompt to run at midnight.
What task should you include in your solution?
explanation:
Why option C – “Run the prompt” – is the correct choice
The requirement is to execute a prompt automatically at a specific time (midnight). In Microsoft 365 Copilot
the only action that actually triggers the prompt’s execution is the Run operation.
Scheduling a run can be done through Power Automate or Copilot Studio by configuring a recurring trigger
(e.g., “Recurrence – Daily at 00:00”). The task therefore consists of running the stored prompt at the
scheduled time.
The other options address ancillary steps but do not fulfill the execution requirement:

Create an agent – an agent is a conversational container; it does not schedule or invoke the prompt.
Create a notebook – notebooks are used for data exploration and model training; they are unrelated to
prompt execution scheduling.
Save the prompt – saving merely persists the prompt definition; without a run, the prompt never fires at
midnight.
Hence, the essential task to meet the midnight-execution need is to run the prompt as part of a scheduled
workflow.
Key points for a certification-style justification
The prompt must be executed at a defined time → requires a run action.
Scheduling logic resides outside the prompt itself; it is supplied by a workflow engine (Power Automate,
Copilot Studio).
“Run the prompt” directly maps to the task of triggering the prompt at the desired moment, making it the only
viable answer.
References
Schedule and run prompts in Power Automate: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/power-
automate/scheduled-flows
Copilot Studio – Run prompts programmatically: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-
365copilot/develop/run-prompts-programmatically
These documents confirm that executing (running) a prompt is the mechanism used to schedule its execution
at specific times such as midnight.

prompt:
For each of the following statements, select Yes if the statement is true. Otherwise, select No.
NOTE: Each correct selection is worth one point.
explanation:
Statement 1: Detect offensive text in images stored in SharePoint $\rightarrow$ YesHow it works: Microsoft
Purview Communication Compliance uses integrated Optical Character Recognition (OCR) processing
alongside advanced Azure AI classifier models. When active, it scans not only standard text strings within
email or Teams chat feeds, but it also extracts and parses text embedded directly inside graphical image files
(such as .png, .jpg, or .tiff screenshots) uploaded to connected Microsoft 365 storage locations like
SharePoint Online and OneDrive for Business to flag workplace conduct violations.
Statement 2: Anonymizes user identities by default $\rightarrow$ YesHow it works: To ensure fairness,
prevent reviewer bias, and satisfy strict data privacy regulations (such as GDPR) during internal compliance
audits, Purview implements pseudonymization by default.The Investigation Layer: When a policy triggers an
alert on a suspicious message, the dashboard initially obfuscates the sender's actual display name, email, and
metadata, replacing them with generic placeholders (e.g., Anonymized User 1). Only users specifically granted
the highly restricted Communication Compliance Admins or investigation privacy override roles can explicitly
request to unmask and view the real user identities after an initial review.
Statement 3: Adds a disclaimer to monitored communications $\rightarrow$ NoHow it works: Microsoft
Purview Communication Compliance operates entirely as a passive, backend monitoring and pattern-matching
evaluation service. It does not actively intercept, append text, or inject visual disclaimers into live user emails,
Teams channels, or chat sessions. Notification and corporate tracking consent are typically handled via
organization-wide login banners, HR policies, or standard legal notice footers configured via Exchange
transport rules, rather than through the Purview compliance scanning engine itself.

Statement 1: Block specific websites $\rightarrow$ YesHow it works: Under Microsoft 365 Copilot web

settings, Active Directory/Entra policies, or Edge management templates. By defining specific domains or
URLs within the Allowed/Blocked sites metadata lists, administrators prevent the LLM engine from retrieving
or indexing content from prohibited public web addresses during real-time retrieval-augmented generation

can look up information on the public web using the Bing search service to answer questions requiring real-

prompt:
You need to ensure that users can use an external system as a knowledge source for custom Microsoft 365
Copilot agents.
What should you configure in the Microsoft 365 admin center? To answer, select the appropriate settings in the
answer area.
explanation:
1. Agents
In the context of Microsoft 365 Copilot, Agents (formerly known as Copilot extensions or specialized Copilots)
are tools that extend the capabilities of Copilot by connecting it to specific knowledge bases or automating
niche workflows.
Management: This tab allows admins to view, enable, or disable specific agents developed within the
organization or acquired via the Microsoft AppSource.
Customization: It provides oversight for agents created in Microsoft Copilot Studio, ensuring they adhere to
organizational policies before being deployed to end-users.
Purpose: Agents allow Copilot to act as a "specialist" for certain departments, such as a HR agent that
specifically understands company leave policies better than the general model.
2. Connectors
Connectors are the technical bridges that allow Microsoft 365 Copilot to access data residing outside of the
standard Microsoft 365 tenant (like SharePoint or OneDrive).
Data Ingestion: Using Graph Connectors, Copilot can index data from external sources such as ServiceNow,
Salesforce, SQL databases, or enterprise file shares.
Grounding: Once connected, this external data is included in the Microsoft Graph. This allows Copilot to
"ground" its answers in non-Microsoft data, providing a more comprehensive response to user prompts.
Governance: The Connectors tab in the admin center is used to monitor the health of these data connections,
manage crawl frequencies, and ensure that external data is being ingested securely.

prompt:
Your company plans to use Microsoft 365 Copilot.
You need to provide a user with the ability to use Microsoft 365 Copilot, including the Researcher and Analyst
agent.
What should you use?
explanation:
Justification
Option A – Microsoft 365 admin center: This is the central control plane for provisioning Microsoft 365
Copilot licenses and assigning them to users (including the Researcher and Analyst agents). Licensing
assignments made here automatically enable the embedded Copilot experiences within Outlook, Teams,
Word, Excel, etc. Admins can also verify compliance and health of the service from this console.
Option B – Microsoft Purview portal: Primarily used for data governance, classification, retention, and
eDiscovery. While Purview may oversee the data sources that Copilot consumes, it does not provision or
assign the Copilot license itself.
Option C – Microsoft Entra admin center: Focuses on identity and access management (e.g., users, groups,
conditional access). Although Entra is involved in authentication, it does not host the Copilot licensing
configuration.
Option D – Defender portal: Dedicated to security posture, threat detection, and response across
Microsoft 365 services. It does not manage Copilot licensing or agent capabilities.
Therefore, the only place where you actively assign the Microsoft 365 Copilot license and enable the
Researcher and Analyst agents is the Microsoft 365 admin center.
References
Microsoft 365 admin center – Manage Copilot licenses: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-
365/admin/setup/copilot-license?view=o365-worldwide-2024
Assign Copilot licenses in the admin center: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/copilot/assign-
license?view=o365-worldwide-2024

prompt:
Your company is evaluating Microsoft 365 Copilot pay-as-you-go billing instead of purchasing a Microsoft 365
Copilot license.
In which scenario can pay-as-you-go billing apply?
explanation:
Technical justification
Option C – “using a custom agent that is grounded in work data” is the only scenario that triggers the
consumption-based, pay-as-you-go billing model. Custom agents execute on-demand workloads that are
metered (e.g., number of prompts, compute cycles, or data processed). Each interaction incurs a usage
charge, which is exactly what the pay-as-you-go option is designed to cover.
Option A – “performing multi-step reasoning by using the Researcher agent” relies on built-in, pre-defined
reasoning capabilities that are included in the standard Microsoft 365 Copilot license. These capabilities are
not metered separately; they are covered by the fixed-price license.
Option B – “generating a recap of a Microsoft Teams meeting” is also a native Copilot feature that comes
with the licensed subscription. It does not involve separate consumption tracking, so pay-as-you-go does not
apply.
Option D – “using the AI assistant to edit a document in Copilot in Word” likewise uses standard Copilot
functionality bundled with the license, not a usage-based service.
Therefore, only the custom-agent scenario aligns with the variable-cost pricing model.
References
Microsoft Learn: Pay-as-you-go pricing for Microsoft 365 Copilot (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-
us/microsoft-365/copilot/pricing?view=o365-worldwide)
Microsoft Docs: Custom agents in Copilot (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/copilot/custom-
agents?view=o365-worldwide)

prompt:
Your company has a Microsoft SharePoint site named Site1. Site1 contains all the policies of the company’s HR
department. The policies are saved as Microsoft Word documents.
All users have read access to Site1.
The HR department manager reports that user requests about the policies are NOT being addressed in a timely
manner, especially around major holidays.
You need to recommend a solution to enable the users to find the HR department policies. The solution must
provide the users with a list of common queries and ensure that responses are grounded only in Site1.
What should you include in the recommendation?
explanation:
Technical justification
Correct choice – B. a custom Microsoft 365 Copilot agent
A custom Copilot agent can be bound to a specific SharePoint site (Site1) and trained on the Word policy
documents stored there.
It can surface a list of frequently asked questions, retrieve policy excerpts, and generate answers strictly
from the site’s content, ensuring that responses are “grounded” only in Site1.
The agent can be reused across users, scaled for holiday-season traffic spikes, and managed centrally by the
HR admin team.
Why the other options are less suitable
A. the personal assistant in Copilot in Word – This feature assists a single user while authoring or editing a
document; it does not provide a site-wide query interface or manage a repository of policy documents.
C. the Researcher agent in Microsoft 365 Copilot – Researcher is designed for exploratory research across
an organization’s data and generates insights, not for delivering structured, rule-based policy answers to
end-users.
D. a Microsoft 365 Copilot notebook – Notebooks are interactive, developer-oriented workspaces; they lack
built-in user-facing query handling and cannot be delivered as a managed service for end-users.
References
Custom Copilot agents in SharePoint: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365-copilot/custom-
agents-sharepoint
Manage Copilot in Microsoft 365: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/copilot/manage-copilot
These resources outline how to create a SharePoint-bound Copilot agent and administer it for consistent,
policy-centric responses.
